items.creator: Bob Dylan
items.title: Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again
items.quote:
It all seems so well timed

An’ here I sit so patiently

Waiting to find out what price

You have to pay to get out of

Going through all these things twice
items.reference: Bob Dylan, “Stuck Inside of Mobile with the Memphis Blues Again,” 1966. Written by Bob Dylan.
items.comment: I read the lines as a warning about timing because futures work often succeeds or fails in relation to a moment: when a scenario, warning, or intervention arrives, who is ready to hear it, and what institutions can still do with it. Dylan’s speaker notices that everything seems “so well timed,” but the cost appears when one assumes that the same passage can be repeated and escaped in the same way. In futures studies, this means a well-timed success should not become a formula: contexts shift, consequences accumulate, and going through the same things twice may carry a different price.
items.commentTitle: Why I read this as futures-studies wisdom

items.creator: Dylan
items.title: It's All Over Now, Baby Blue
items.quote:
The highway is for gamblers, better use your sense
Take what you have gathered from coincidence
The empty-handed painter from your streets
Is drawing crazy patterns on your sheets

referenceParts.text: It's All Over Now, Baby Blue

items.comment: We should not gamble on the future but deliberate about possible futures, even though all we have for that deliberation are scattered fragments gathered, almost by coincidence, from the past. Scholars are therefore “empty-handed painters”: they try to understand the obscure patterns of the future—or perhaps can only draw necessarily obscure descriptions of what might be possible.
items.commentTitle: Why I read this as futures-studies wisdom
